elements.sent-2411-001: In 1957, Steinhaus proved that a chain of regular tetrahedra, meeting face-to-face and forming a closed loop does not exist \cite{steinhaus1957 .}

elements.sent-2411-010: Since ancient times mathematicians have been fascinated by the existence of polyhedra.
elements.sent-2411-011: The Platonic solids are well-known examples of such polyhedra whose properties have been the focus of many studies.
elements.sent-2411-012: For instance, geometric and combinatorial properties of regular polyhedra, i.e.\ polyhedra whose faces are regular polygons, are well established, see \cite{onetriangle,symmetry,ghentpaper,coxeterhelix,eppstein,deltahedra .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Nevertheless, the properties of regular and irregular polyhedra continue to inspire a wide range of investigations.
elements.sent-2411-014: In this paper, we study a weakened version of the Steinhaus conjecture using the theory of simplicial surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-015: We recall a question summarising this conjecture from \cite{steinhaus1957 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Is there a finite sequence of congruent regular tetrahedra $T_1,\ldots, T_n $ such that for $i=1,\ldots,n$ the tetrahedra $T_i$ and $T_{i+1$ meet face-to-face? (We read the subscripts modulo $n$.).}
elements.sent-2411-017: Here, two congruent tetrahedra $T,T'\subseteq \mathbb{R ^3$ are said to meet face-to-face if the intersection $T\cap T'$ is a triangle that forms a face of both $T$ and $T'$.}
elements.sent-2411-018: In the literature, a sequence that arises from iteratively glueing regular tetrahedra together is often referred to as a chain of regular tetrahedra.
elements.sent-2411-019: Two examples of such chains consisting of three and six regular tetrahedra are illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:tetrahedrasequence .}
elements.sent-2411-020: We call such a chain of regular tetrahedra perfect, if a face of the last tetrahedron coincides with a face of the first one.

elements.section-2411-004: Relation between clusters of wild tetrahedra and simplicial surfaces
elements.sent-2411-187: In this section, we establish a relationship between clusters of wild tetrahedra and simplicial surfaces which underpins our investigations.
elements.sent-2411-188: More precisely, we compute a simplicial surface that represents a partial combinatorial structure of a given cluster of wild tetrahedra.
elements.sent-2411-189: We shall refer to such a simplicial surface as a multi-tetrahedral surface, see Definition~\ref{definition:relation .}
elements.sent-2411-190: Additionally, we elaborate on the advantages of using simplicial surfaces to describe clusters of wild tetrahedra.
elements.sent-2411-191: For instance, these multi-tetrahedral surfaces facilitate a complete description of the corresponding clusters of wild tetrahedra.
elements.sent-2411-192: By exploiting the notion of the tetra-symbol, we assign a simplicial surface to a cluster of wild tetrahedra.
elements.sent-2411-193: Let $\tau=(T_1,\ldots,T_n)$ be a cluster of wild tetrahedra and $\mathcal{V _{\tau =\{p_1,\ldots,p_m\}$ be the set of vertices of the given cluster.}}
elements.sent-2411-194: We recall from Definition~\ref{defintion:tetrahedron that the sets of all edges and faces of $\tau$ are denoted by $\mathcal{E _\tau$ and $\mathcal{F _\tau$, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-195: For integers $1\leq i<j<k\leq m,$ we define the number $\eta_{i,j,k}$ as follows: If $\conv(\{p_i,p_j,p_k\})\notin \mathcal{F}\tau,$ we define $\eta_{i,j,k :=0.$ If $\conv(\{p_i,p_j,p_k\})$ forms a face in $\mathcal{F _\tau,$ then we define $\eta_{i,j,k}$ as the number of tetrahedra containing this face as a subset.}}
elements.sent-2411-197: If the set \begin{equation}X_\tau =\{1,\ldots,m\} \cup \{\{i,j\}\mid \conv(\{p_i,p_j\}) \in E_{\tau}\} \cup \{\{i,j,k\}\mid \eta_{i,j,k}=1\}\end{equation} satisfies the axioms of a simplicial surface, we call this surface a multi-tetrahedral surface.
elements.sent-2411-198: Note that by excluding the $3$-subsets $\{i,j,k\}$ with $\eta_{i,j,k =2,$ we remove the faces that share two tetrahedra in a cluster of wild tetrahedra.}
elements.sent-2411-199: We call $X_{\tau}$ a multi-tetrahedral sphere, if the simplicial surface $X_{\tau}$ satisfies $\chi(X_\tau)=2.$ We say that the multi-tetrahedral surface $X_{\tau}$ is proper if the cluster $\tau$ is a chain.
elements.sent-2411-200: We refer to the surface $X_{\tau}$ as a multi-tetrahedral torus if the surface satisfies $\chi(X_{\tau )=0.$.}
elements.sent-2411-201: For instance, with the above remark, we can exploit the tetra-helices and double tetra-helices defined in Section~\ref{subsection:chainsoftetra to obtain proper multi-tetrahedral spheres with corresponding embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-202: Different clusters of wild tetrahedra can give rise to isomorphic multi-tetrahedral surfaces, see Definition~\ref{def:isomorphic .}
elements.sent-2411-203: Furthermore, if the edge lengths of the wild tetrahedra of the given cluster differ, then the edge lengths induce a wild-colouring of the corresponding multi-tetrahedral surface.
elements.sent-2411-204: It can be shown that up to the permutation of colours, if a wild-colouring of a multi-tetrahedral surface exists, then it is unique, see \cite{fowler .}
elements.sent-2411-205: Furthermore, we know that for a given multi-tetrahedral sphere, there exists exactly one wild-colouring.
elements.sent-2411-206: In this paper, multi-tetrahedral surfaces are defined as the partial incidence geometries of clusters of wild tetrahedra.
elements.sent-2411-207: However, in \cite{simplicialsurfacebook an alternative approach to define multi-tetrahedral spheres is used.}
elements.sent-2411-208: In that context, multi-tetrahedral spheres are defined by iteratively attaching tetrahedra to a given simplicial tetrahedron without removing faces which occur in two tetrahedra.
elements.sent-2411-209: For more information on multi-tetrahedral spheres, we refer to \cite{maRey,simplicialsurfacebook .}
elements.sent-2411-210: In this section, we study the embeddings of the wild-coloured simplicial tetrahedra that result in wild tetrahedra.
elements.sent-2411-211: Investigations dealing with the existence of wild tetrahedra in $\R^3$ satisfying certain edge length restrictions have been addressed in different studies.
elements.sent-2411-212: For instance, in \cite{acuteangleold and \cite{menger the authors establish that a wild tetrahedron with non-co-planar vertices has acute-angled triangles as faces.}}
elements.sent-2411-213: Additionally, in \cite{ansgar Strzelczyk shows that up to isometries there exists exactly one wild tetrahedron with edge lengths $(a,b,c)$ if and only if $(a,b,c)$ gives rise to a non-obtuse-angled triangle.}
elements.sent-2411-214: As described above, these publications focus on the existence and uniqueness of tetrahedra with respect to their edge lengths.
elements.sent-2411-215: Here, we describe a wild tetrahedron by providing explicit coordinates for the vertices of the given tetrahedron.
elements.sent-2411-216: These coordinates facilitate an easy description of the wild tetrahedra and thereby enable us to conduct more efficient computations in Maple.

elements.sent-2411-236: Next, we describe a procedure to embed a wild-coloured multi-tetrahedral surface into $\R^3$.

elements.sent-2411-376: The computations to obtain the desired toroidal polyhedra are all carried out using the computer algebra systems GAP and Maple.
elements.sent-2411-377: The GAP-package SimplicialSurfaces gives us functionalities to examine the combinatorial structure of the simplicial surfaces in this paper.

elements.sent-2411-380: We computed the mentioned census as described in the following: First, we use GAP to extract proper multi-tetrahedral spheres from established databases of multi-tetrahedral spheres (see \cite{brinkmann ).}
